title: Intergenerational class mobility and the convergence thesis: reflections 25 years later
abstract: The extraordinary trio of papers by Robert Erikson, John H. Goldthorpe and Lucienne Portocarero in the British Journal of Sociology in 1979, 1982, and 1983 advanced thinking and research in the field of social stratification and mobility. Their agenda became the agenda of the whole research committee on stratification and mobility within the International Sociological Association (known as RC28) for over a decade thereafter. The agenda was to quantify mobility similarities and differences over time and between nations and to use the results to test hypotheses about the social sources of those differences. The 1983 paper reproduced here announced that agenda and its results anticipated the main conclusions of the research that followed. Comparative mobility research of the late 1970s and early 1980s grew out of three new resources. Large-scale studies of men's mobility patterns were conducted in five nations – France, Great Britain, the USA, Poland, and Hungary each provided high-quality data. Statistical work by Leo A. Goodman (collected in Goodman 1984) and Otis Dudley Duncan (1979) aided the enterprise. A bold and creative paper by Featherman, Jones, and Hauser (1975) comparing the American data with a smaller Australian data set provided a precise hypothesis of constant social fluidity – the FJH hypothesis – to orient work. Featherman, Jones, and Hauser also contributed a useful decomposition of mobility patterns into structural and circulation components that many researchers subsequently found useful.1 Erikson, Goldthorpe, and Portocarero used these tools – the French and British surveys, Goodman's models, and Featherman, Jones, and Hauser's ideas – to address both classical and contemporary questions. To marshall these resources, Erikson, Goldthorpe, and Portocarero needed one more tool: a class schema specific enough to capture distinctive structural features of each society under comparison and rooted in the classical concerns that motivated class analysis. The result – universally known now as the ‘EGP schema’ and operationalized in almost 100 different data sets – served not only their purposes but those of the rest of the field.2 Significantly, and perhaps instructively, their major substantive contribution depends on a humbler tool – their much smaller survey of Swedish men' s mobility. French, British, and American mobility patterns turn out to be quite similar in the Erikson, Goldthorpe, and Portocarero papers and others' analyses. The analytical leverage came from the distinctive patterns in the little Swedish data set. The tools in place, the substantive question for Erikson, Goldthorpe, and Portocarero was whether each society converges over time toward a common destiny or moves on its own historical path. The convergence thesis of the title supposed that ‘industrial society’ is a type and each nation manifests an expression of the single latent logic of that type. In the alternative view, history matters. In the difference between Swedish mobility patterns and those of France and Britain, Erikson, Goldthorpe, and Portocarero (1979) found the first clues that history mattered. But that paper featured static comparisons of contemporary mobility patterns and, thus, could not settle the question whether Sweden was different because its late industrialization had allowed it to skip some phase of greater inequality of opportunity typified by France and Britain or if it was persistently different. Note how the first finding was already a huge challenge for the universalist point of view. The late industrializing Sweden did not ‘lag behind’ Britain and France with some kind of ‘legacy’ high correlation between social class origins and destinations. It had somehow mysteriously skipped ahead to the lower association that was the logical conclusion of the universalism built into industrial society. With their cohort comparisons in the 1983 paper, Erikson, Goldthorpe and Portocarero dismantled that jerry-rigging, too. I quote from p. 338 at length: [T]he data we have analysed can provide no indication of any continuous movement in relative rates in any direction, and the observed shifts that do in fact imply increases in fluidity or openness would seem better interpreted as sporadic and short-term phenomena rather than as forming part of some comprehensive developmental tendency. (Erikson, Goldthorpe and Portocarero 2010 [1983]: 338) In place of an inexorable progress toward universalism, Erikson, Goldthorpe, and Portocarero substituted a stable, hard to alter core of unequal class relations. A sophisticated blend of Featherman-Jones-Hauser and Weber, they directed attention to ‘the distribution of class advantage and power and the structure of class relations’ typical of industrial society. Some nations – prototypically Sweden – pursue a politics that alters the distribution of class advantage and power by arraying welfare state institutions against market forces. Others – prototypically the USA – pursue a different politics that abets market forces (Esping Anderson 1990; DiPrete 2002). The subsequent book by Erikson and Goldthorpe (1992) broadened the evidence base and strengthened the argument. Research by others (e.g., Vallet 2001) turned up evidence that France changed considerably more than this paper showed. Much of Europe also changed from the 1970s to the 1990s (Breen and Luijkx 2004). The 1983 paper also focused exclusively on men's mobility. Extensive research on women's chances (Hout 1988; Breen and Luijkx 2004) has yet to turn up gender differences nearly as big as those between British and Swedish men. In short, the subsequent research enriched our descriptions of class relations, but none has shaken the fundamental insight that welfare-state politics frames the inequality of opportunities and outcomes. Research on intragenerational mobility over the last fifteen years strengthens the case in favor of the original insights of Erikson, Goldthorpe, and Portocarero. Social democratic welfare states promote the equal outcomes and smooth over economic dislocations better than states that expose workers to market forces (Hout and DiPrete 2004). They achieve this in part by facilitating continuity in women's careers (DiPrete 2002), but they also buffer the personal impact of disruptions like unemployment and retirement (e.g., Gangl 2006). This research programme, carried out by a community of scholars shaped and informed by Erikson and Goldthorpe's many projects, dispatched the view dominant when Erikson and Goldthorpe began and came to agree with their reading of the political economy of inequality. In particular, the prevailing view now harks back, as Erikson, Goldthorpe, and Portocarero did, to the 1927 conclusion of Sorokin – cited in the closing sentences of Erikson, Goldthorpe, and Portocarero (1983) – that the pressures in favour of stratification push classes apart rather continuously while the countervailing forces of equalization tend rather to be expressed more ‘convulsively’. To the extent to which research has advanced on that conclusion, it has been to identify the continuous processes of inequality with market forces, especially labour market forces, and the convulsive countervailing factors with political events and politically supported institutions.
